records.habitat: Alvar grasslands, bedrock shores, and open calcareous habitats.
records.reason: Rare native wild populations in Michigan; this entry is about naturally occurring populations, not ordinary garden chives.
records.field_marks: Round hollow onion-scented leaves from bulbs; pink to lilac flowers packed in an upright rounded umbel.
records.look_alikes: Garden chives and other onion-like Allium species. Habitat matters: the rare entry is the wild plant in the right natural setting.
records.care_note: Do not share exact spots from natural populations.

records.habitat: Rich northern woods, cool calcareous forest, and other mesic sites with base-rich soils.
records.reason: A localized northern sedge with limited Michigan distribution.
records.field_marks: Broad arching sedge leaves in clumps, with drooping seed heads on slender stalks; looks more graceful and open than many stiff woodland sedges.
records.look_alikes: Other broad-leaved woodland sedges, especially species of rich northern forest. Fruiting structure is the safer separator.
records.care_note: Best handled as a sedge for careful photography rather than collecting.

records.habitat: Cold northern bog margins, wet mossy ground, and other boreal-style wetlands.
records.reason: A northern wetland buttercup with limited Michigan occurrence.
records.field_marks: Low creeping buttercup with glossy yellow flowers and rounded to kidney-shaped leaves on long petioles.
records.look_alikes: Other small buttercups of wet ground. Check for the low creeping habit and northern bog setting.
records.care_note: Wetland plants are easy to damage underfoot; photograph from the edge when possible.

records.habitat: Dunes, rocky barrens, cold shorelines, and acidic open northern ground.
records.reason: A low northern evergreen shrub that is uncommon and habitat-tied in Michigan.
records.field_marks: Mat-forming evergreen shrub with tiny needle-like leaves and black berry-like drupes.
records.look_alikes: Low heath-family shrubs such as bearberry; black fruit and needle-like leaves help.
records.care_note: Slow-growing mats can be damaged easily by trampling.

records.habitat: Moist meadows, northern openings, and calcareous wet ground.
records.reason: A showy gentian with a limited Michigan footprint.
records.field_marks: Deep blue trumpet-like flowers with narrow opposite leaves spaced up the stem.
records.look_alikes: Other blue gentians; the long narrow leaves and more open stem are useful clues.
records.care_note: Flowering photos are the easiest way to document it cleanly.

records.habitat: Open sandy woods, clearings, and dry northern ground.
records.reason: Local and easily overlooked in Michigan despite being more familiar farther west.
records.field_marks: Silvery-gray narrow leaves and clusters of papery white everlasting flower heads.
records.look_alikes: Pearly everlasting and other composite flowers with white heads; the silvery foliage is a useful clue.
records.care_note: Photograph leaf color and the whole plant, not just the flower head.

records.habitat: Rich northern woods, cool slopes, and mesic hardwood forest.
records.reason: A delicate northern lily relative with a restricted Michigan range.
records.field_marks: Arching stem with hanging greenish to cream bell-like flowers and later orange to red berries; leaves clasp the stem in a layered look.
records.look_alikes: Other fairy-bells and bellwort-like woodland plants. Fruiting stems and leaf arrangement help.
records.care_note: Stem architecture is easy to miss in close-up shots; include a full-plant image.

records.habitat: Cold seeps, stream edges, springs, and wet open ground.
records.reason: Localized seep and wet-edge populations make it worth tracking carefully in Michigan.
records.field_marks: Bright yellow snapdragon-like flowers, often with reddish spotting in the throat, on soft green wet-ground plants.
records.look_alikes: Other yellow wetland flowers, but the monkey-flower blossom shape is distinctive up close.
records.care_note: Wet seep habitat is fragile; avoid stepping into the seep itself.

records.habitat: Moist conifer-hardwood forest floor, cedar woods, and rich northern duff.
records.reason: Tiny moonwort species with sparse populations and easy-to-miss stature.
records.field_marks: Very small moonwort with a single divided sterile blade and a separate fertile spike; often only a few inches tall.
records.look_alikes: Other moonworts and tiny sterile juvenile ferns. Good close photos of both blade and fertile segment matter.
records.care_note: This one is easy to overlook and easy to crush.

records.habitat: Cool mossy conifer forest, cedar-spruce woods, and other intact boreal-style forest.
records.reason: One of the most charismatic rare orchids in the north woods, but extremely vulnerable to disturbance.
records.field_marks: Single pink to rosy orchid flower with a slipper-like lip, yellow beard, and one basal leaf.
records.look_alikes: Other small orchids are possible, but the flower is distinctive when fresh.
records.care_note: Do not dig, transplant, or publicize exact spots.

records.habitat: Cold northern shoreline, rocky openings, and subboreal habitat pockets.
records.reason: A northern aster with very limited Michigan occurrence.
records.field_marks: Lavender to pale purple aster heads with yellow centers on a relatively low northern plant of cool open habitat.
records.look_alikes: Other asters; habitat and overall plant size help narrow it down.
records.care_note: Include leaves and whole-plant habit in photos, not only the flower.

records.habitat: Open Great Lakes dune systems and sandy shore habitat.
records.reason: A classic Great Lakes dune specialist with sensitive shoreline populations.
records.field_marks: Silvery-gray spiny thistle with woolly leaves and a pale to pinkish thistle head; usually in open sand.
records.look_alikes: Other thistles, especially non-native shore thistles. The silvery dune-adapted look is important.
records.care_note: Dune habitat is extremely easy to damage; keep locations vague.

records.habitat: Old hardwood logs, dead standing beech or maple, and mature northern forest.
records.reason: Old-forest associated tooth fungus with localized northern records; a strong candidate for a U.P. fungi watchlist.
records.field_marks: White coral-like branching mass on wood, with hanging teeth or spines from the branchlets instead of gills or pores.
records.look_alikes: Other Hericium species and coral fungi. This one grows directly from wood and carries dangling teeth.
records.care_note: Older fruitbodies yellow and brown, so the best ID window is when fresh and pale.

records.habitat: Mature hardwood forest, especially on large dead hardwood logs or trunks.
records.reason: A large northern tooth fungus tied to intact deadwood habitat and worth tracking separately from the more commonly noticed Hericium species.
records.field_marks: White cascading cluster of long dangling teeth from a compact central mass on hardwood.
records.look_alikes: Lion’s mane and coral tooth. This one forms a denser hanging mass rather than a broad coral spray.
records.care_note: Photograph both the fruitbody and the wood it is attached to.

records.habitat: Mature conifer or mixed northern forest, often in cool mossy ground.
records.reason: Often treated as uncommon to rare in parts of North America and worth tracking carefully in northern forests.
records.field_marks: Fan- to vase-shaped fruitbody with lilac to violet tones when fresh; underside has thick wrinkled ridges rather than true gills.
records.look_alikes: Chanterelle relatives and odd vase fungi. Check for purple tones and blunt wrinkled folds.
records.care_note: Usually associated with conifers in cool northern forest.

records.habitat: Boreal conifer forest, mossy ground, and cold spruce-influenced litter.
records.reason: A striking northern fungus tied to cool intact habitat and often treated as rare or highly local.
records.field_marks: Dark brown to black gelatinous cup or barrel on the forest floor, often appearing like a small cauldron filled with shiny dark jelly.
records.look_alikes: Black cups and odd spring ascomycetes. The thick barrel-like form is the clue.
records.care_note: Most often reported from spruce-influenced forest floor litter.

records.habitat: Conifer forest with specialized soil and ectomycorrhizal communities.
records.reason: Associated with older conifer systems and not something people report every day in the U.P.
records.field_marks: Young fruitbodies are pale and velvety and often exude striking red droplets; underside has teeth instead of gills or pores.
records.look_alikes: Other tooth fungi, especially older brown hydnoid species.
records.care_note: The dramatic red droplets fade; older specimens become much less obvious.

records.habitat: Cool conifer forest and acidic northern ground with specialized fungal communities.
records.reason: A striking hydnoid fungus of northern woods and a reasonable watchlist target for intact conifer systems.
records.field_marks: Blue to blue-gray cap tones when fresh, with a toothed underside instead of pores or gills.
records.look_alikes: Other Hydnellum and Phellodon species. Fresh color and the overall chunkier fruitbody help.
records.care_note: Older specimens fade toward gray-brown, so young fresh fruitbodies are much easier to call.

records.habitat: Undisturbed mossy forest floor or grassland pockets with low nutrient disturbance.
records.reason: An unmistakable violet coral fungus that is uncommon enough to justify a watchlist in sensitive northern habitat.
records.field_marks: Branched coral fungus with vivid lilac to violet color through much of the fruitbody.
records.look_alikes: Paler coral fungi and faded older violet coral. Strong fresh purple coloration is the giveaway.
records.care_note: Color fades quickly with age and drought; photograph fresh material when possible.

records.habitat: Conifer forest floor, often in mossy acidic ground with mature tree partners.
records.reason: A northern stipitate tooth fungus that fits the kind of specialized forest-floor watchlist species people easily miss.
records.field_marks: Gray to dark cap with a toothed underside and a central stalk, often in small clustered groups on the forest floor.
records.look_alikes: Other Phellodon and Hydnellum species. The stalked habit and thinner gray cap help.
records.care_note: Needle litter and moss should be photographed with the mushroom because habitat context matters.
